does: This paper identifies brittleness in LLM safety alignment where models refuse harmful requests in standard prompts but comply with the same intent under adversarial rewording. The authors propose that robust safety requires context-invariant alignment—where refusal decisions depend on underlying intent rather than surface form. They introduce Anchor Invariance Regularization (AIR), a training method that uses verifiable prompts (like multiple-choice questions with reliable feedback) as anchors and applies stop-gradient regularization to pull open-ended variants toward matching the anchor's safety behavior. This asymmetric approach avoids the pitfall of symmetric invariance regularizers, which degrade performance on reliable variants instead of improving robustness. The method is implemented as a plug-in technique compatible with standard preference-based post-training alignment approaches.
connects: This work builds on preference-based post-training methods like RLHF and DPO that align LLMs with human values, addressing a known limitation: safety behavior often fails under distribution shift or adversarial prompt rephrasing. It responds to emerging research on adversarial robustness in language models and the gap between refusal behaviors across prompt variations. The paper draws on invariance regularization from domain adaptation and causal inference literature, where enforcing consistency across contexts improves generalization. It also engages with recent work distinguishing between reward model brittleness and genuine alignment failures, recognizing that different training signals carry different reliability levels. The core tension—balancing between verifiable and noisy feedback sources—extends discussions in the mechanistic interpretability and reward modeling communities about trustworthiness of supervision signals.
enables: This approach opens avenues for more robust safety evaluation frameworks that leverage verifiable prompts as ground truth anchors. It suggests future work on identifying or constructing reliable safety baselines for other domains beyond multiple-choice settings. The method points toward hybrid alignment schemes that exploit heterogeneous feedback quality, potentially extending to other downstream tasks requiring consistency. It enables research into when and why surface-form variations fool safety mechanisms, supporting interpretability work on what safety-relevant features models actually learn. The asymmetric regularization principle could generalize beyond safety to other domains where some contexts admit cleaner labels than others, such as factuality or instruction following. Future work might explore automatic anchor identification or learning which prompt variants deserve stop-gradient treatment.
